original_abstract: As international student mobility evolves into a multibillion-dollar global enterprise, universities are increasingly turning to third-party agents and commission-based recruiters to bolster their international enrollments. This article examines the troubling implications of this trend, highlighting the inherent conflicts of interest and ethical dilemmas that arise when student placement is driven by financial incentives rather than academic suitability. By analyzing the risks of unregulated recruitment—including the potential for credential fraud and the lack of objective guidance for prospective students—the authors argue against the commercialization of the admissions process. Finally, the article outlines strategic recommendations for universities and governments to provide transparent, direct, and student-centered support that better serves the long-term interests of both the institutions and the global student community.
humanized_abstract: When we think of globalHigher Education, few people think of money, but when they look at the numbers they can see why tertiary institutions are looking to third parties to recruit international students who will pay the fees. The commercialisation of student recruitment, although a multi-billion dollar business, carries with it a number of problems and ethics challenges. This paper sets out the problems of agents and brokerage and the dangers of fraud and the mis-leading of students through an unregulated market place. The article concludes with a number of recommendations to institutions and governments to facilitate access to tertiary education for all students by means of fair, open and transparent procedures directly linking students with a quality tertiary education, to the advantage of all.
